_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
**1. Biosecurity and Vehicle Decontamination Systems (ASF)**
Scope: All road freight operations entering, transiting, or departing Estonia.
Requirement: Implement a documented vehicle disinfection programme aligned with PTA (Agriculture and Food Board) guidance. ASF spreads via contaminated vehicles, footwear, and equipment; there is no treatment or vaccine, and culling is the only response once detected. The workspace must establish a standard operating procedure (SOP) covering: pre-trip vehicle inspection checklists, mandatory disinfection station stops on Estonian rural routes, driver footwear and clothing hygiene protocols, and incident reporting chains to PTA.
Deadline: Immediate — disease-control measures are active following the October 2025 formal end of the emergency phase.
Gap from baseline: If no Estonia-specific ASF biosecurity SOP exists, this is a gap requiring closure within 30 days.

**2. Energy Tariff Monitoring and Procurement Review**
Scope: All workspace facilities with electricity consumption in Estonia and the Baltic states.
Requirement: Establish an energy data monitoring process to track the impact of ENMAK 2035 market liberalisation and the reverse auction programme on electricity tariffs. The ministry projects prices falling from 9 cents/kWh to 6.6 cents/kWh by 2030. Workspaces operating warehouses, cold chain, event logistics infrastructure, or data-intensive tracking systems in Estonia should model procurement scenarios under the liberalised market from 2025–2030.
Deadline: Ongoing; price impacts begin materialising as new wind capacity enters the market (onshore farms targeted for completion by end 2029).
Gap from baseline: If energy procurement contracts are locked at pre-liberalisation rates, a renegotiation window review is recommended.

**3. Baltic Sea Routing Compliance Monitoring**
Scope: Ocean freight operations through the Gulf of Finland, Northern Baltic Proper, and Gulf of Riga.
Requirement: Subscribe to Estonian Maritime Administration Notices to Mariners for updates on MPA designation in the Estonian EEZ (approximately 11,421 km²). Once the Government of the Republic formally designates restricted navigation zones, boundaries and restrictions must be published in Notices to Mariners per the Exclusive Economic Zone Act. Workspace ocean mode operators or charterers must build a monitoring process to capture these designations.
Deadline: No statutory deadline yet published for MPA designation; monitoring infrastructure should be in place before any government order is issued.
Gap from baseline: If no Baltic EEZ routing review process exists, one must be established.

**4. Wind Energy Zone Land-Use Conflict Screening**
Scope: Logistics sites, last-mile hubs, or event infrastructure near Estonian rural or coastal areas.
Requirement: The Estonian Maritime Spatial Plan and the National Spatial Plan 2050 designate 1,783 km² of wind energy development zones. Municipalities receiving Wind Speed Bonus pre-funding are actively enabling wind farm construction. Workspace site selection, lease renewals, or new facility planning must screen for proximity to designated wind zones and any associated access restrictions or planning conditions.
Deadline: National Spatial Plan 2050 under preparation since 2023; wind zone designations in the Maritime Spatial Plan are already effective.
Gap from baseline: If no spatial conflict screening is embedded in facility planning, this is a process gap.

**5. Climate-Resilient Economy Act Sector Classification Watch**
Scope: All Estonian operations.
Requirement: Monitor the legislative progress of the draft Climate-Resilient Economy Act, which will set sectoral GHG reduction targets for 2030, 2035, 2040, and 2050. If freight/logistics is classified as a regulated sector, the workspace will face reporting obligations on GHG emissions, potentially including Scope 1 (vehicle fleet) and Scope 3 (supplier) emissions. Over 900 amendments were proposed during public consultation; the law continues in government review as of Q1 2026.
Deadline: Passage timeline uncertain; legal monitoring required quarterly.
Gap from baseline: No GHG sector classification impact assessment for Estonian operations has been confirmed in existing documentation.

*Source: Ministry of Climate, Climate-Resilient Economy Act, https://kliimaministeerium.ee/en/climate-resilient-economy-act; Estonian Exclusive Economic Zone Act, Riigi Teataja, https://www.riigiteataja.ee/en/eli/ee/520012015003/consolide; ENMAK 2035, Ministry of Climate, January 2026, https://kliimaministeerium.ee/en/news/estonian-government-approved-development-plan-make-energy-sector-more-reliable-affordable-and.*
